lesson.name: Plato: Dialogues & the Republic - Lesson 4 — Symposium

meta.lessonObjective: Love, beauty, and the ascent to the Good.

tasks.chapterTitle: The Speeches of Phaedrus and Pausanias
Aristophanes on the Origin of Love
Agathon and the Character of Love
Socrates and the Wisdom of Diotima

messages.content:
Lesson 4 — Symposium - Presentation26 Slides
The Highest Love Seeks Abstraction
  • The highest love is the love not of a person, but of the highest and purest abstraction.
  • This abstraction is the far-off heaven on which the eye of the mind is fixed in fond amazement.
Test your understanding of this lesson:

questions.question: According to the introduction what does the highest love seek instead of a person?
questions.explanation: The text states the highest love is the love not of a person but of the highest and purest abstraction.

questions.question: What does Diotima say Love is between the mortal and the immortal?
questions.explanation: She describes Love as neither mortal nor immortal but in a mean between the two as a great spirit.

questions.question: From what particulars does the ascent described in the text begin?
questions.explanation: We rise by steps proceeding from the particulars of sense to the universal of reason.

questions.question: What quality does the perfect vision of beauty possess according to the supplied text?
questions.explanation: We arrive at the perfect vision of beauty not relative or changing but eternal and absolute.

questions.question: Why is Love always in a mean between ignorance and knowledge?
options: Because no god or wise man seeks wisdom
Because he is the son of Zeus
Because he is young and tender
questions.explanation: No god is a philosopher for he is wise already nor does any man who is wise seek after wisdom.

cards.front: What is the highest love according to the introduction?
cards.back: The love not of a person but of the highest and purest abstraction.

cards.front: How does the text describe the movement of the ascent?
cards.back: By a regular series of steps from particulars of sense to the universal of reason.

cards.front: What character does the final vision of beauty have?
cards.back: Eternal and absolute not relative or changing.

cards.front: What is Love according to Diotima?
cards.back: A great spirit intermediate between the divine and the mortal.

cards.front: From what parents is Love born?
cards.back: Plenty the son of Discretion and Poverty on the birthday of Aphrodite.

cards.front: Why does Love occupy the mean between ignorance and knowledge?
cards.back: No god seeks wisdom for he is wise already and no wise man seeks wisdom.

cards.front: What does the soul gain through the ascent?
cards.back: Participation in the eternal nature and the true beauty or good.

cards.front: What does Eryximachus say harmonious love produces?
cards.back: Health and plenty for men animals and plants.

cards.front: What does Aristophanes identify as the ancient desire?
cards.back: The desire of one another implanted in us reuniting our original nature.

cards.front: What does Agathon call the source of every good in heaven and earth?
cards.back: The birth of Love and the Love of the beautiful.

slides.title: The Highest Love Seeks Abstraction
bullets: The highest love is the love not of a person, but of the highest and purest abstraction.
This abstraction is the far-off heaven on which the eye of the mind is fixed in fond amazement.
Once fixed upon that abstraction the mind ceases to chase the shifting images of beauty in bodies or laws and instead draws its life from the single source that lends them all their borrowed light.
Yet the ascent leaves the lover inwardly alone for no earthly attachment can any longer claim the allegiance once given to the eternal object of thought.
slides.notes: Drawn from introduction summary of the doctrine.

slides.title: Love Penetrates Philosophy
bullets: The same passion which may wallow in the mire is capable of rising to the loftiest heights of penetrating the inmost secret of philosophy.
Plato includes in his doctrine of love the unity of truth and the consistency of the warring elements of the world.

When this same eros is disciplined by dialectic it carries the soul step by step from the beauty of one body to the beauty of laws and sciences until the lover finally beholds the form of the beautiful itself and is thereby changed into a philosopher.

The reconciliation love effects among warring elements therefore does not remain private; it compels the knower to return to the city and refashion its conflicts into a single ordered whole that mirrors the unity already grasped in contemplation.
slides.notes: Prepares the ascent theme from the supplied introduction.

slides.title: The Eye of the Mind Fixed on Beauty
bullets: The enthusiasm for knowledge when first beaming upon mankind belongs to the doctrine of love.
The relativity of ideas to the human mind and of the human mind to ideas forms part of this teaching.

When the mind fixes its gaze upon beauty, the love that first stirred its desire for knowledge now lifts that gaze from fleeting images to the single form in which all loveliness coheres, so that knowing itself becomes an act of beholding the unchanging.

This ascent, however, carries an inner tension: the same relativity that binds mind and idea means the eye can slip back into shadows unless love continually rekindles the effort to keep the beautiful steadily in view.
slides.notes: Continues the summary of love's philosophical reach.

slides.title: Faith in the Invisible and Eternal
bullets: The faith in the invisible and the adoration of the eternal nature are included in Plato's doctrine of love.
These elements prepare the mind for the vision that transcends changing particulars.

Through this inward turn the lover discovers that every beautiful thing draws the soul upward only when it is recognized as a sign of something higher, so that ordinary desire is gradually refined into a steady longing for the source itself.

The mind thus steadied gains the power to endure the disappearance of beloved particulars without despair, because its true allegiance has already passed to the eternal Good that remains when all else changes.
slides.notes: Focuses on the eternal aspect central to the objective.

slides.title: Rising by Regular Steps
bullets: We rise from one to the other by a regular series of steps or stages proceeding from the particulars of sense to the universal of reason.
From one universal to many we finally reunite in a single science.

Each new stage therefore demands that the lover relinquish exclusive attachment to any single beautiful body or law, accepting instead the widening horizon that alone can steady the soul against the pull of mere appearance.

Only when the ascent reaches its final unity does the seeker discover that every earlier step was secretly governed by the Good, whose radiance now makes the whole ladder intelligible and turns scattered loves into one coherent life.
slides.notes: Direct description of the ascent process.

slides.title: From Sense Particulars to Universal Reason
bullets: The ascent begins with the particulars of sense and moves steadily toward the universal of reason.
This movement connects the visible world to the intelligible realm of beauty.
Each beautiful sight therefore acts as a ladder rung that the lover must climb and then leave behind, lest attachment to one body or face arrest the soul short of its true destination.

The same movement that begins in erotic attraction thus ends by reordering the entire soul, so that reason, once awakened, governs desire and directs it toward the single source from which all beauty flows.
slides.notes: Expands the step-by-step progression.

slides.title: The Perfect Vision of Beauty
bullets: At last we arrive at the perfect vision of beauty not relative or changing but eternal and absolute.
This beauty is not bounded by this world or in or out of this world but an aspect of the divine.

Once the soul has seen this absolute beauty it can finally measure every changing thing against a standard that never wavers, and therefore no longer mistakes fleeting images for the real object of desire.
Yet the same vision that liberates also binds the philosopher to return and speak of what he has seen, knowing that those still chained below will at first call his words madness rather than truth.
slides.notes: Core statement of the goal of the ascent.

slides.title: Beauty Extending Over All Things
bullets: The perfect vision extends over all things and has no limit of space or time.
It is the highest knowledge of which the human mind is capable.
From this vantage every scattered instance of beauty appears as a participation in one unchanging source, so desire ceases to wander among fragments and gathers itself into a single love of the Good.
Yet the mind that reaches this height must still inhabit a body bound by time, creating the lasting tension that the soul now sees eternity while daily life continues to press its partial claims.
slides.notes: Emphasizes the unlimited character of the final vision.

slides.title: Immortality Through the Ascent
bullets: At first immortality means only the succession of existences and even knowledge comes and goes.
Then follows in the language of the mysteries a higher and higher degree of initiation.
The soul that reaches this stage no longer seeks to preserve itself through children or poems but stands face to face with the single unchanging form that beauty truly is.
Yet the same ascent that grants lasting vision also compels the lover to descend again into the city, carrying what he has seen back to those still bound by lesser images.
slides.notes: Shows how the ascent transforms the meaning of immortality.

slides.title: The Cause of All Things
bullets: Plato is willing to rest in the contemplation of the idea which to him is the cause of all things.
He has no strength to go further than this vision of the eternal.
From this single idea every particular beauty borrows its power to attract the soul, turning scattered desires into one steady ascent that ends only when the mind rests in the source itself.
The vision therefore marks both the limit and the fulfillment of human striving, since nothing beyond the eternal cause can be thought or loved without losing the ground of its own existence.
slides.notes: Links the ascent to the idea as cause.

slides.title: Pausanias Distinguishes Two Loves
bullets: There are two loves as there are two Aphrodites one the daughter of Uranus who is the elder and wiser goddess.
The other is the daughter of Zeus and Dione who is popular and common.
The elder love therefore draws the soul upward toward lasting excellence and shared understanding, so that true lovers become better together rather than merely satisfying momentary appetite.
By contrast the common love remains bound to the body and changes with every passing attraction, leaving those who follow it vulnerable to jealousy and regret once desire cools.
slides.notes: Sets up the distinction between higher and lower love.

slides.title: Heavenly Love Has Noble Purpose
bullets: The first of the two loves has a noble purpose and delights only in the intelligent good.
This heavenly love points toward the ascent rather than remaining with earthly objects.

When the soul yields to this love it begins to generate not children of the body but the lasting offspring of wisdom and law, so that the lover becomes a maker of virtue both in himself and in others.

Yet this ascent demands that the lover relinquish the comfort of single beautiful things, accepting instead the labor of moving from one to many and finally to the single form that makes all beauty intelligible.
slides.notes: Connects Pausanias to the higher path.

slides.title: Eryximachus on Harmonious Love
bullets: When the elements of hot and cold moist and dry attain the harmonious love of one another they bring health and plenty.
The wanton love getting the upper hand is the source of pestilence and many other diseases.

The physician therefore becomes a servant of harmonious love, using his art to reconcile opposing qualities within the body so that health emerges as an active and stable condition rather than a fragile accident.

When wanton love gains ground, the resulting diseases do not remain private misfortunes; they weaken the very capacities for measured action and perception that allow a person to move toward the beautiful and the good.
slides.notes: Shows harmony as a model for ordered ascent.

slides.title: Love in Music and Seasons
bullets: In music as in medicine both loves ought to be noted for they are both present.
The course of the seasons is also full of both these principles of love.

When the nobler love rules music, opposing tones are drawn into measured concord, and the resulting harmony awakens in the listener a longing that lifts the mind beyond mere sound toward intelligible beauty.

So too, when the measured love governs the turning year, the clash of summer heat and winter frost is tempered into timely succession, and this visible rhythm of growth and rest quietly trains the soul to recognize an ordered ascent toward the Good.
slides.notes: Extends the principle of ordered love across domains.

slides.title: Aristophanes on Desire of the Whole
bullets: Each of us when separated having one side only is always looking for his other half.
The intense yearning which each has toward the other is the desire of something the soul evidently desires and cannot tell.

This restless search therefore turns every embrace into a momentary restoration of wholeness, yet it also breeds the fear that the chosen partner may prove only an imperfect match for the soul's original form.
As a result the lover often discovers that physical closeness alone cannot quiet the deeper ache, leaving the pair bound together by a longing that neither can fully name or satisfy.
slides.notes: Presents the original human desire that the ascent fulfills.

slides.title: Zeus Plan to Heal Human Division
bullets: Zeus turned the parts of generation round to the front so that by mutual embraces man and woman might breed and the race continue.
If man came to man they might be satisfied and rest and go their ways to the business of life.
This frontward turn therefore binds reproduction to desire so that every embrace carries the hidden consequence of new life while still leaving the deeper hunger for wholeness unsatisfied.

The same device grants same-sex unions a temporary peace that returns each partner to ordinary affairs yet quietly preserves an opening for bonds strong enough to lift the soul beyond the body toward lasting good.
slides.notes: Explains the ancient desire that love seeks to satisfy.

slides.title: Agathon on Love Dwelling in Softness
bullets: Love walks not upon the earth nor upon the skulls of men but in the hearts and souls of both gods and men which are of all things the softest.
He is the tenderest as well as the youngest and also he is of flexile form.

Because love refuses every surface hardened by pride or injury, the soul must first soften its own defenses if it hopes to receive him at all, turning private tenderness into the first step toward shared beauty.
His yielding nature means he never forces an entrance yet once inside he reshapes whatever receives him, so that pleasure felt between two people can quietly become the soul’s training for the good.
slides.notes: Highlights beauty and tenderness as attributes leading upward.

slides.title: Love as Source of All Good
bullets: Since the birth of Love and from the Love of the beautiful has sprung every good in heaven and earth.
Love is the fairest and best in himself and the cause of what is fairest and best in all other things.

When love first awakens through the sight of beauty it does not rest content with any single lovely thing but presses onward, drawing the soul step by step from bodies to laws, from laws to knowledge, until every human endeavor is touched by order and measure.

Therefore the city that honors love as its hidden source finds its citizens striving for excellence without coercion, while the city that forgets this source soon discovers that courage, justice, and even wisdom lose their warmth and become brittle.
slides.notes: Positions love as origin of beauty and good.

slides.title: Diotima Corrects Socrates on Love
bullets: Love was neither fair nor good but in a mean between them.
Love is neither mortal nor immortal but in a mean between the two as a great spirit.
Because love stands between the fair and the good it must forever reach for what it lacks, so every meeting with beauty becomes a ladder that draws the soul upward rather than a resting place.
As the great spirit bridging mortal and immortal realms love alone can carry human longing into the presence of the divine and return with the guidance that makes the ascent to the good possible.
slides.notes: Begins Diotima's teaching that refines prior speeches.

slides.title: Love as Intermediate Daimon
bullets: Love interprets between gods and men conveying and taking across to the gods the prayers and sacrifices of men.
Through Love all the intercourse and converse of God with man whether awake or asleep is carried on.
Because this ceaseless carrying between realms belongs to love alone, the human soul reaches the divine only when love is active, and without it every attempt at prayer or vision remains unanswered.

The same office therefore places love itself in perpetual tension, neither fully god nor fully man, so that the lover is driven onward in search of the beauty that would finally satisfy the longing love has awakened.
slides.notes: Defines Love's mediating role in the ascent.

slides.title: Birth of Love from Poros and Penia
bullets: On the birthday of Aphrodite Plenty fell into a heavy sleep and Poverty lay down at his side and conceived Love.
As his parentage is so also are his fortunes always poor yet always plotting against the fair and good.
This inheritance leaves Love forever resourceful in its need, turning every lack into a clever scheme that draws it toward whatever is beautiful and worthy.
The same double origin therefore makes Love the living bond between human poverty and divine fullness, so that the soul is driven onward rather than allowed to settle short of the Good.
slides.notes: Gives the mythic origin that explains Love's character.

slides.title: Love a Philosopher at All Times
bullets: Love is a mighty hunter always weaving some intrigue keen in the pursuit of wisdom fertile in resources.
He is by nature neither mortal nor immortal but alive and flourishing at one moment and dead at another.

Because love shifts constantly between flourishing and fading the philosopher must treat every moment of insight as a summons to climb higher binding transient beauty to the unchanging Good before it slips away.

This in-between condition therefore turns every pursuit of wisdom into an unending ascent where the lover risks losing all partial gains unless he keeps pressing toward the single source that never dies.
slides.notes: Shows Love as the embodiment of philosophical striving.

slides.title: No God or Wise Man Seeks Wisdom
bullets: No god is a philosopher or seeker after wisdom for he is wise already.
Nor does any man who is wise seek after wisdom for wisdom is the end of the ascent.
The lover of wisdom therefore occupies the middle realm where lack itself becomes the living bond that draws the soul upward through visible beauty toward the unseen Good.
Without that felt incompleteness neither mortal striving nor the ordered ascent would ever begin, for possession leaves nothing further to desire.
slides.notes: Clarifies why Love must occupy the intermediate realm.

slides.title: The Soul Capable of Eternal Nature
bullets: That the soul has such a reach of thought and is capable of partaking of the eternal nature seems to imply that she too is eternal.
Enough for Plato to have attained the true beauty or good without enquiring precisely into the relation in which human beings stood to it.
Because the soul can reach the unchanging, its love of visible beauty must serve as the first step in an ascent that gradually detaches it from becoming and unites it with what truly is.
The absence of any detailed account of embodiment therefore leaves the philosopher free to treat the Good as an object of direct vision rather than as a possession belonging to this or that person.
slides.notes: Concludes the ascent with the soul's participation in the eternal.
